metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare Finding 3 as an explicit dependency (or prerequisite) for AC6, specify what the evidence artifact contains when this story ships standalone (e.g. wheel/sdist digests recorded by this story's own build step as a fallback), and note that the Finding 3 integration is conditional on that story landing first.
findings.finding: AC6 (release evidence artifact) includes 'the build-once step (Finding 3) appends the wheel/sdist SHA-256 sums' — this is an undeclared cross-ticket dependency (VCDT #9). The AC can only be fully satisfied after Finding 3 lands; yet the plan declares no dependency edge, sequencing requirement, or fallback for the case where this story ships before Finding 3.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6 ('the build-once step (Finding 3) appends the wheel/sdist SHA-256 sums')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the step-ordering enforcement of the OIDC co-residency guarantee with a job-split: perform curl download + sha256sum verification in a separate job that does NOT carry `id-token: write`, then execute `mcp-publisher login github-oidc` and `mcp-publisher publish` in the existing `mcp_registry` job (which retains `id-token: write`) — or document and scope-down the stated guarantee to reflect that step ordering is a defence-in-depth convention, not a structural barrier.
findings.finding: The plan states 'the `id-token: write` (OIDC) scope is never co-resident with an unverified binary' and enforces this purely through step ordering (download → verify → execute within the same job). In GitHub Actions, `id-token: write` is a job-level permission: the OIDC token endpoint ($ACTIONS_ID_TOKEN_REQUEST_URL) is available to every process running in the job from its first step, regardless of step sequence. A compromised archive could call the token endpoint during the download step, before verification runs. Step ordering is a convention, not a structural enforcement boundary, so the stated security contract cannot actually be satisfied by the proposed mechanism. A job-split — download and digest-check in a job without `id-token: write`, then execute in a separate job that requires `id-token: write` — is the structurally correct mechanism to enforce the co-residency guarantee the plan names.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher paragraph ('the `id-token: write` (OIDC) scope is never co-resident with an unverified binary'); AC5; release.yml mcp_registry job (lines 117–119, 127–134)
findings.suggested_fix: Split the `mcp_registry` job into two: `mcp_verify` (no `id-token: write`; runs curl download + sha256sum check + sets a job output `verified: true`) and `mcp_registry` (needs `mcp_verify`; has `id-token: write`; copies the verified binary via artifact or re-downloads with `--no-clobber` using the already-validated hash). Alternatively, if a full split is deemed too heavy, update the plan's language to correctly scope the guarantee as 'step-ordered defence-in-depth' rather than a structural 'never co-resident' contract — and remove the AC5 claim that this is a security isolation boundary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC7, e.g. a grep that asserts `docs/releasing.md` contains the required section headings: `grep -q '## Pinning' docs/releasing.md && grep -q 'pin-update' docs/releasing.md` (or equivalent), runnable in the same workflow-lint step as AC1/AC2.
findings.finding: AC7 ('`docs/releasing.md` documents the pinning policy and pin-update process') names a deliverable but provides no concrete proving command — no grep/check is specified that would produce a pass/fail signal in CI or in a review. A reviewer cannot deterministically confirm the required sections exist without an explicit check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the workflow-lint step to run: `grep -qE '^## Pinning' docs/releasing.md || (echo 'Missing Pinning section in docs/releasing.md' && exit 1)`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge that `gerrit-verify.yaml` itself contains an un-SHA-pinned `actions/setup-python@v6` at line 75 (contradicting its header comment); either scope the new lint to `release.yml` only (explicitly, not as an 'extension') or include fixing `gerrit-verify.yaml`'s own gap in this story's scope.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The repo already SHA-pins third-party actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml` (per its header comment)' and bases the AC lint on this being an extendable existing convention. However, `gerrit-verify.yaml` line 75 contains `actions/setup-python@v6` — a tag pin, not a SHA — directly contradicting the header comment ('All third-party actions are SHA-pinned'). The existing SHA-pin coverage is only partial (non-`actions/*` third-party actions like `lfreleng-actions/*` and `im-open/*` are SHA-pinned; `actions/setup-python@v6` is not). The AC's lint is therefore not 'extendable' from a complete existing convention — it must be newly created, and the gap in `gerrit-verify.yaml` itself is not acknowledged or scoped.
findings.location: Why section ('The repo already SHA-pins third-party actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml`') and AC line 1 ('extendable from the repo's existing SHA-pin convention in `gerrit-verify.yaml`')
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC, replace 'extendable from the repo's existing SHA-pin convention in `gerrit-verify.yaml`' with 'a new workflow-lint grep step targeting `release.yml` (note: `gerrit-verify.yaml` line 75 also has an unpinned `actions/setup-python@v6` — include or explicitly exclude that fix from this story's scope).'  In the Why section, narrow the premise to 'The repo SHA-pins some third-party actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml`' rather than asserting it is comprehensive.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the canonical lock file path (e.g. `.github/release-requirements.txt`) as the plan's committed default rather than 'e.g. under `.github/` or `requirements/`', and replace the `<lock>` placeholder in AC3's proving command with that exact path.
findings.finding: The release build lock/constraints file location is given as 'e.g. under `.github/` or `requirements/`' — both an E2 defaultable gap (executor must choose) and an E6 proving-command defect: AC3's proving command literally reads `pip install --dry-run --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>`, where `<lock>` is an unresolved placeholder path. Because the path appears in the CI step and in the lint command, any executor who picks the 'wrong' location will produce a mismatched proving command.
findings.location: Scope section ('A new release build lock/constraints file (e.g. under `.github/` or `requirements/`)') and AC3 proving command (`-r <lock>`)
findings.suggested_fix: Pick `.github/release-requirements.txt` as the stated default and update AC3's proving command to: `pip install --dry-run --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt`

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'Adopt the uv / pip pinning pattern (uv: …; pip: …)' with terminology that matches the actual tools used — e.g. 'Adopt the pip-compile / pip pinning pattern' — since uv has no role in this codebase's CI and the work is entirely pip-based.
findings.finding: The 'What' section opens with 'Adopt the uv / pip pinning pattern (uv: full-SHA action pins, …; pip: SHA-pinned actions + a hash-locked build environment …)' but uv has no CI workflow footprint in this repo — only a uvx runtime hint in server.json and local-dev docs. The actual work described is entirely pip-based (`pip-compile`, `pip install --require-hashes`). Labeling the pattern 'uv / pip' when uv is not involved in the CI execution path is cargo-cult nomenclature that will confuse the executor and reviewers.
findings.location: What section, first bullet: 'Adopt the uv / pip pinning pattern (uv: full-SHA action pins…)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the opening of the What section with: 'Adopt a pip-compile / pip hash-locking pattern: SHA-pin all action `uses:`, set `persist-credentials: false`, generate a hash-locked build env with `pip-compile --generate-hashes`, and install it with `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps`.' Remove the parenthetical '(uv: …; pip: …)' framing entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an independent verification step for the MCP publisher SHA-256 at pin time: require either (a) a GPG/Sigstore-signed upstream checksums file (document how to verify the signature), or (b) a two-reviewer policy where the second reviewer independently downloads and hashes the binary, or (c) verification against a Sigstore/SLSA provenance attestation if the upstream project provides one — and document the chosen method in `docs/releasing.md` under the pinning-update process.
findings.finding: The MCP publisher SHA-256 pin-time verification process is under-specified: when no upstream signed checksums file exists, the plan says to hard-code 'the SHA-256 computed at pin time' but does not specify HOW that hash must itself be verified independently. A reviewer who fetches the binary on a compromised machine (or fetches an already-compromised release artifact) would enshrine the wrong hash, and subsequent releases would 'verify' it forever — defeating the purpose of the check on this OIDC-privileged boundary.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher subsection; AC line: 'If upstream ships no checksums, the pinned SHA-256 is hard-coded in the workflow with a rationale comment'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the pin-update process in `docs/releasing.md`: 'When updating the MCP publisher pin, two reviewers must independently compute `sha256sum mcp-publisher.tar.gz` from separate download sessions and confirm the hashes match before committing. If the upstream project publishes Sigstore attestations or a GPG-signed checksums.txt, verify the signature first and derive the hash from it rather than from a raw download.' Also add an AC line: 'The `docs/releasing.md` pin-update process requires independent hash verification (two-reviewer or signed-upstream) before committing a new MCP publisher SHA-256.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the ownership contradiction: either remove 'wheel/sdist digests' from this story's 'What' evidence list and explicitly note AC 6 has a hard dependency on Finding 3, OR assign wheel/sdist digest logging to this story and update AC 6 to reflect that (removing the Finding 3 attribution).
findings.finding: The 'What' section lists wheel/sdist digests as part of this story's evidence logging ('Print and retain in release evidence: … the wheel/sdist digests'), but AC 6 attributes wheel/sdist SHA-256 sums to Finding 3's build-once step ('the build-once step (Finding 3) appends the wheel/sdist SHA-256 sums'). An implementer reading 'What' will implement wheel/sdist digest logging here; an implementer reading AC 6 will defer it to Finding 3 — these are contradictory directives.
findings.location: 'What' section (last bullet, 'Print and retain in release evidence') vs AC 6 ('the build-once step (Finding 3) appends the wheel/sdist SHA-256 sums')
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, remove 'and the wheel/sdist digests' from the evidence list with a note '(wheel/sdist digests owned by Finding 3)'. In AC 6, add an explicit prerequisite note: 'NOTE: wheel/sdist digest entries in the evidence artifact depend on Finding 3 being merged first; AC 6 is partially verifiable independently of Finding 3.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out or emergency-override procedure to the plan (e.g., a documented break-glass path for updating the lock file, or an emergency PR process to temporarily bypass the lint step with dual approval) so the irreversibility of --no-build-isolation is matched by a commensurate recovery mechanism.
findings.finding: The --no-build-isolation change is deliberately made irreversible by a lint step that fails if it is removed ('A workflow lint/test step fails if --no-build-isolation is removed — no free-resolution path remains'), but the plan provides no documented back-out procedure for the scenario where the lock file is incorrect, incomplete, or causes build failures in production. The T4 criterion requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan, not just acknowledgement of irreversibility.
findings.location: 'What' section (Hatchling enforcement — decision bullet) and AC 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Back-out / Emergency' subsection to Scope or What: 'If the hash-locked file causes a build failure, the recovery path is: (1) regenerate the lock with pip-compile --generate-hashes and open an emergency PR through the Gerrit two-vote gate with a P0 label; (2) the lint step may be temporarily disabled by a two-reviewer override commit documented in the PR — this path is logged in release evidence.' Document this process in docs/releasing.md under the Pinning section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a lint/grep check (symmetric with the --no-build-isolation lint) that scans release.yml for the presence of `--require-hashes` in the build-tool install command, failing the gate if it is absent — so dropping `--require-hashes` from the install step is caught as quickly as dropping `--no-build-isolation` from the build step.
findings.finding: The --require-hashes enforcement on the release install step is verified only through a post-install dry-run CI step, not through a lint/grep check on the release.yml install command itself. If a future edit drops --require-hashes from the install step, the dry-run (which checks the already-installed environment against the lockfile) would still pass, leaving the newly-defaulted risky path undetected — a proxy-validation gap analogous to the one the plan correctly addresses for --no-build-isolation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — third bullet ('Release build tools … install from a reviewed hash-locked file … verified by a CI step that … runs pip install --dry-run --require-hashes …')
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the existing workflow-lint step (already checking SHA pins, persist-credentials, and --no-build-isolation) to also grep release.yml for `--require-hashes` in the pip install line and fail if absent. Document this in the same AC bullet that currently describes the dry-run check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the MCP publisher 'download → verify → execute' bullet, explicitly reference the existing acli pattern in `external-integration.yml:76-100` as the model, and confirm whether the mcp-publisher step should include the same post-extract binary smoke check (`./mcp-publisher --version`) that the acli pattern uses.
findings.finding: The plan independently specifies a 3-step 'download → verify → execute' pattern for the MCP publisher (curl to file, sha256sum -c, tar xzf) without referencing that this identical pattern already exists in the codebase for the acli binary in `external-integration.yml:76-100` and `reconcile-bridge.yml:150-179`. The plan misses the opportunity to anchor the executor to the existing pattern, risking divergence (e.g., the acli pattern includes a version smoke-check and size/executable validation that the plan omits for mcp-publisher).
findings.location: What section, 'MCP publisher — download → verify → execute (never stream into tar)' bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Out of Scope clause: replace 'pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned' with the accurate scoping rationale (e.g. 'pinning first-party actions in non-release workflows — deferred; this story focuses on the release supply chain specifically').
findings.finding: The Scope section's Out of Scope clause — 'pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned' — is factually inaccurate: grep confirms that _optionality.yml, test.yml, external-integration.yml, mirror-guard.yml, eval.yml, reconcile-bridge.yml, eval-plan-review-container.yml, verify-identity.yaml, terraform-drift.yml, and reconcile-bridge-canary.yml all use `actions/checkout@v7` and `actions/setup-python@v6` (and in some cases `actions/upload-artifact@v7`) without SHA pins. Only third-party actions in gerrit-verify.yaml are SHA-pinned. An executor following this Out of Scope clause as written would correctly limit the change to release.yml, but reviewers seeing the broader codebase may flag the inconsistency, and future plans may incorrectly inherit this false premise.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Out of scope: pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned'
findings.suggested_fix: Change: 'Out of scope: pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned' → 'Out of scope: SHA-pinning first-party actions in non-release workflows (test.yml, gerrit-verify.yaml, _optionality.yml, etc.) — deferred; this story targets the release supply chain only.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` to the Scope file list and specify that the new workflow-lint step's signature must be registered in `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` (or document a deliberate asymmetry in `_INTENTIONAL_SCRIPT_ASYMMETRIES` with a rationale if the lint step intentionally runs only in one gate).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new 'workflow-lint/grep check' CI step that verifies SHA-pins and persist-credentials in release.yml, but does not list `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` as an edit target. That test file enforces (`_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES`) that every CI gate present in test.yml also appears in gerrit-verify.yaml. If the new workflow-lint step is added to test.yml (or gerrit-verify.yaml), test_ci_workflow_parity.py will fail unless the step's signature is also added to `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES`. This is an unenumerated consumer of the new gate step.
findings.location: Scope section (file list) and AC bullet 1 ('verified by a workflow-lint/grep check')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reference and align with the `acli` download→verify→execute pattern in `external-integration.yml:76-100` as prior art; evaluate whether the MCP publisher pin should use repo variables (`MCP_PUBLISHER_VERSION`, `MCP_PUBLISHER_SHA256`) analogously to `ACLI_VERSION`/`ACLI_SHA256` rather than hard-coding values in workflow YAML.
findings.finding: The plan's MCP publisher 3-step download→verify→execute pattern is not traced back to the functionally identical pattern the repo already uses for `acli` in `external-integration.yml:76-100`. That prior art uses repo *variables* (`ACLI_VERSION`, `ACLI_SHA256`) rather than values hard-coded in the workflow YAML, which makes pin updates a repo-settings change rather than a workflow diff — a meaningfully better update ergonomic the plan does not consider.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher: download → verify → execute; external-integration.yml:76-100
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'MCP publisher' bullet of the What section, add: 'Align with the existing acli verification pattern (`external-integration.yml:76-100`). Store the exact version and SHA-256 as repo variables (`MCP_PUBLISHER_VERSION`, `MCP_PUBLISHER_SHA256`) so pin updates are a settings change rather than a workflow diff; fail loudly if either variable is unset or contains a mutable reference like `latest`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve before implementation whether `modelcontextprotocol/registry` GitHub releases ship a `checksums.txt` or `.sha256` sidecar; state the finding in the plan and specify the verification path (upstream manifest preferred, else document the exact procedure for computing and recording the self-computed SHA-256 in a reviewable change).
findings.finding: The plan defers resolution of whether `modelcontextprotocol/registry` GitHub releases ship a `checksums.txt` or `.sha256` file, saying 'Prefer an upstream checksums.txt if … ships one' — leaving an unverified capability assertion that is load-bearing for the verification approach (it determines whether the SHA-256 comes from a signed upstream manifest or a value computed once by the implementer at pin time).
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher bullet: 'Prefer an upstream checksums.txt if `modelcontextprotocol/registry` ships one; otherwise hard-code the SHA-256'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align AC 3's verification language with the tool choice: either commit to one tool (recommended: pick pip-compile for simplicity) and remove the alternative from 'What', or update AC 3 to accept either tool ('generated by pip-compile --generate-hashes or uv pip compile --generate-hashes').
findings.finding: The 'What' section presents two equally valid tools for generating the hash-locked file — 'pip-compile --generate-hashes (pip-tools) (or uv pip compile --generate-hashes)' — but AC 3's verification step specifies only 'generated by pip-compile --generate-hashes', silently ruling out the uv alternative without justification. An implementer choosing uv would satisfy the 'What' intent but fail the AC's stated verification criterion.
findings.location: 'What' section (hash-locked file bullet) vs AC 3
findings.suggested_fix: In 'What', remove '(or uv pip compile --generate-hashes)' and commit to pip-compile. In AC 3, keep 'generated by pip-compile --generate-hashes' as-is. Add a note: 'uv may be substituted in a future story if the toolchain is standardized on uv.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--max-time <N>` (e.g. 120 s) and `--retry 3 --retry-delay 5` to the MCP publisher `curl` step so slow/hung downloads fail fast and retry before giving up, rather than blocking the runner indefinitely.
findings.finding: The curl download step for the MCP publisher specifies `-fsSL` (fail on HTTP error) but no `--max-time` or `--connect-timeout` flag. If the exact-version download URL is slow or the TCP connection hangs, the release runner will block indefinitely — holding the OIDC-capable job open with no timeout or automatic cancellation.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher step 1: `curl -fsSL "<exact-version-url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `curl -fsSL "<exact-version-url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz` with `curl -fsSL --max-time 120 --retry 3 --retry-delay 5 "<exact-version-url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz`. Document the chosen timeout value in the workflow comment alongside the pinned URL and SHA-256.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `pip-compile --generate-hashes` (pip-tools) and/or `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` flag names from a live `--help` invocation, confirm which tool will be installed in CI (adding it explicitly to the workflow bootstrap step), and record the confirmed command in the plan before implementation begins.
findings.finding: `pip-compile --generate-hashes` (pip-tools) is introduced as the primary lock-file generation command, but pip-tools is not present in the repo's dev dependencies (`pyproject.toml`) or any existing workflow, and the flag name `--generate-hashes` has not been verified from a live `pip-compile --help` invocation. The plan also offers `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` as an alternative, which is likewise unverified. A flag-level mismatch (e.g. the flag is `--with-hashes` in some versions, or the command is `pip-compile` vs `pip compile`) would cause a silent or hard CI failure on first use.
findings.location: What section — 'Create a reviewed release lock/constraints file … generated and maintained with an established tool — pip-compile --generate-hashes (pip-tools) (or uv pip compile --generate-hashes)'
findings.suggested_fix: Run `pip-compile --help | grep hash` locally (pip-tools ≥ 6.x uses `--generate-hashes`) and `uv pip compile --help | grep hash` (uv uses `--generate-hashes` since 0.1.x); add the confirmed invocation and the pip-tools install step (e.g. `pip install pip-tools`) to the plan's What section so the executor has a verified command string.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `pip --version` precondition check (≥ 22.1) to AC3's verification step description, or replace `--dry-run` with `--report /dev/null` (available since pip 22.2) and confirm the chosen flag from a live `pip install --help` before committing the AC.
findings.finding: AC3 specifies `pip install --dry-run --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>` as the verification command, but the `--dry-run` flag for `pip install` was not verified from a live `pip --help` invocation. It was introduced in pip 22.1; Ubuntu runners carry pip ≥ 22 today, but the plan does not state a minimum pip version precondition, and the flag is Unverified from live observation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'a CI step that, against the already-installed environment, runs `pip install --dry-run --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>`'

findings.finding: AC2 ('Every release checkout sets persist-credentials: false') has no described proving command — no grep, lint assertion, or CI check is specified to verify this property, unlike AC1 which names a lint/grep check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'Every release checkout sets persist-credentials: false'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC2: 'verified by the same workflow-lint step used for AC1, which also greps for `persist-credentials: false` on every `actions/checkout` block in `release.yml` and fails if absent'.

findings.finding: The AC for build-tool version verification specifies `pip list --format=freeze > tool-versions.txt` and 'a CI lint step asserting the installed versions match the pinned lock exactly (a diff against the lock's pinned versions must be empty)', but `pip list --format=freeze` emits `Package==version` lines with no hashes, while `pip-compile --generate-hashes` lock files contain hash annotations and inline comments — the formats are structurally incompatible for a direct diff, so the 'diff must be empty' assertion as written will never pass without a normalization step that is not specified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Release build tools install from a reviewed hash-locked file… a CI lint step asserting the installed versions match the pinned lock exactly (a diff against the lock's pinned versions must be empty)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a diff against the lock's pinned versions must be empty' with: 'a CI check that runs `pip install --dry-run --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>` against the already-installed environment exits zero, confirming all installed versions satisfy the locked hashes.' This is directly implementable and does not require format normalization.

findings.finding: AC5's verification method ('a CI check that substituting a different archive for the pinned one causes the step to exit non-zero') is described as an outcome but not as a concrete step, script, or test job — leaving an executor without a clear implementation target for the mutation test.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5: 'verified by a CI check that substituting a different archive...'

findings.finding: AC6 ('Release evidence records all action SHAs, build-tool versions, the MCP publisher version + digest, and the wheel/sdist digests') names four distinct evidence items but provides no concrete proving command to verify all four are present and captured — tool-versions.txt (from AC3) covers build-tool versions, but action SHAs, MCP publisher digest, and wheel/sdist digests have no specified capture step or artifact assertion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6: 'Release evidence records all action SHAs, build-tool versions, the MCP publisher version + digest, and the wheel/sdist digests'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The repo already SHA-pins actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml` and `_optionality.yml`' as the established convention that `release.yml` is 'inconsistent' with — but probing shows both files use tag-based references for first-party GitHub actions (`actions/setup-python@v6` at gerrit-verify.yaml:75; `actions/checkout@v7` and `actions/setup-python@v6` throughout `_optionality.yml`). The SHA-pin convention in those files applies only to third-party actions (`lfreleng-actions/*`, `im-open/*`). This mischaracterizes the existing convention, which could mislead the implementer about (a) the scope of the lint check they need to write (is `actions/checkout` expected to be SHA-pinned already, or is this story adding that?), and (b) whether lint infrastructure already exists that can be extended.
findings.location: Why section — 'The repo already SHA-pins actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml` and `_optionality.yml`, so `release.yml` is inconsistent with the repo's own convention.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'The repo already SHA-pins actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml` and `_optionality.yml`' with 'The repo SHA-pins third-party actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml` (per its header comment) — this story extends that convention to all action references (including first-party `actions/*`) in `release.yml`.' Update the AC-1 lint-check description to explicitly state it must flag `actions/checkout`, `actions/setup-python`, `actions/upload-artifact`, and `actions/download-artifact` tag refs, not only third-party action tags.

findings.finding: The MCP publisher SHA-256 hard-coding mechanism is underdetermined for multi-arch runners: the existing workflow constructs the download URL from `uname -s` and `uname -m` (producing distinct binaries per OS/arch), so there is not one SHA-256 but one per OS+arch pair. The plan says to 'hard-code the SHA-256 computed at pin time' without specifying how to handle the per-arch dimension — leaving an implementer to either hard-code only linux/amd64 (breaking silently on arm64 runners) or maintain a multi-arch SHA map with no specified structure.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP publisher — download → verify → execute' / release.yml line 129 / AC line: 'the pinned SHA-256 is hard-coded in the workflow with a rationale comment'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the dynamic arch-detection with a pinned linux/amd64 URL (since ubuntu-latest runners used by release.yml are currently amd64) and document this as a deliberate scope decision with a revisit trigger when GitHub enables arm64 ubuntu-latest by default; OR add a small associative-array SHA map keyed by `${OS}_${ARCH}` so every supported arch has a verified entry and the step fails if no entry exists for the detected arch.

findings.finding: The plan specifies pinning the MCP publisher to "an exact version URL" with a single hard-coded SHA-256, but the current download in `release.yml:129` is architecture-dynamic (`uname -s` / `uname -m` with sed transforms produce arch-specific filenames like `mcp-publisher_linux_amd64.tar.gz`). A single URL and single digest can only cover one OS/arch tuple. If the runner arch or OS changes (e.g. GitHub migrates ubuntu-latest to arm64, or macOS runners are added to this job), the pinned URL and digest will not match the runner's binary — causing either a download 404 or a sha256 mismatch. The plan does not state that the pin is intentionally arch-fixed (x86_64/linux only) nor does it specify that per-arch URL+digest pairs must be stored.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher 'download → verify → execute' steps; release.yml:129
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the 'What' section: 'The exact-version URL and pinned SHA-256 are arch-specific; store one `<url, sha256>` pair per supported OS/arch tuple (currently only `linux_amd64` for `ubuntu-latest`) and select the matching pair via a `case $(uname -s)/$(uname -m)` block. Update all pairs when bumping the publisher version.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
